data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Figma Smashes Circle's Record For Best Billion-Dollar IPO Pop
data.description:
A handful of IPOs and SPACs debuted this past week, led by software unicorn Figma (FIG). The company soared +250% in the best first day for a billion-dollar IPO ever, a record just set by cryptocurrency play Circle (CRCL) in June.

Increasing its range earlier in the week, Figma priced above the high end to raise $1.2 billion at a nearly $20 billion market cap. Originally a browser-based tool for designing user interfaces, Figma now offers eight products, including four that were launched in 2025. The company has delivered strong growth, and it has a sticky customer base, over three-quarters of which use multiple Figma products. Figma continued to climb after its historic first day, finishing the week up 270%.

Ambiq Micro (AMBQ) upsized and priced above the midpoint to raise $96 million at a $459 million market cap. The company provides ultra-low power semiconductor solutions designed to address the significant power consumption challenges of general purpose and AI compute, especially at the edge. Ambiq shipped more than 42 million units in 2024, and it estimates that over 40% of them ran AI algorithms. The company was well received on day one and finished the week up 65%.

Medtech Shoulder Innovations (SI) priced well below the range to raise $75 million at a $327 million market cap. Shoulder Innovations is a commercial-stage medical technology company that currently offers advanced implant systems for shoulder arthroplasty. Its "ecosystem" is comprised of these systems, as well as enabling technologies, efficient instrument systems, specialized support, and surgeon-to-surgeon collaboration. The company will have to spend heavily on sales and marketing to maintain its growth. Shoulder Innovations finished down 5%.

Taiwanese carbon fiber parts maker J-Star Holding (YMAT) priced at the low end to raise $5 million at a $68 million market cap. The company primarily produces bicycle and racket parts. J-Star finished up 12%.

7 IPOs During the Week of July 28th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 08/01

Blank check company targeting the critical minerals sector.

Blank check company led by SPAC veterans targeting "high potential" businesses in the US.

TechCreate Group (TCGL)

 $11M Technology Revere Sec.
Distributor of software solutions in Southeast Asia.

Week Ahead

Four IPOs are currently scheduled to list in the week ahead, as the regular summer drop in IPO activity looks set to begin. Some more small names may join the calendar throughout the week.

Space and defense technology provider Firefly Aerospace (FLY) plans to raise $599 million at a $5.8 billion market cap. Firefly Aerospace provides mission solutions to national security, government, and commercial customers. The company's platform includes Launch and Spacecraft Solutions. Within Launch, Firefly has two offerings built on common technologies: Alpha, its operational launch vehicle that has completed four launches successfully; and Eclipse, a reusable and scaled up version of Alpha, which is in final development in partnership with Northrop Grumman and is expected to launch as early as 2026. Its Spacecraft Solutions include Blue Ghost Lander and Elytra, which are highly maneuverable spacecraft enabled by high performance rocket engine technology. The company has a backlog of over $1 billion, and peers have traded up recently. However, Firefly remains unprofitable, and just five of its customers account for 92% of its backlog.

Heart imaging software provider Heartflow (HTFL) plans to raise $200 million at a $1.4 billion market cap. Heartflow offers a non-invasive solution for diagnosing and managing coronary artery disease (CAD). As of March 31, 2025, the Heartflow Platform has been used to assess CAD in more than 400,000 patients, including 132,000 in 2024 alone. The platform creates a personalized 3D model of a patient's heart and deliver actionable insights on blood flow, stenosis, plaque volume, and plaque composition.

Cloud services provider WhiteFiber (WYFI) tentatively plans to raise $125 million at a $558 million market cap in the week ahead. The company, carved out of Bit Digital, provides artificial intelligence infrastructure solutions, stating that its high-performance computing (HPC) data centers provide cloud-based services that rely on NVIDIA GPUs. The company expanded significantly last year with its acquisition of Canada-based Enovum for C$63 million, and this year has expanded its Montreal-area operations, in addition to recent plans to build out a North Carolina data center. WhiteFiber experienced a 84% growth in its cloud services business in the 1Q, but primarily relies on smaller, less-established, potentially less sticky customers.

Japan-based web gaming platform CTW Cayman (CTW) is tentatively scheduled to raise $17 million at a $347 million market cap. The company’s main HTML5 platform, G123.jp, offers a selection of free-to-play games inspired by popular Japanese animations. The company currently has 28 games on its platform and 9 games in pre-registration.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 7/31/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 5.9% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 8.6%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Astera Labs (ALAB) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 29.8% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 21.6%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and J&T Global Express.

data.title: Prysmian S.p.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Prysmian S.p.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Inches Up In July But Remains Historically Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment inched up in July, increasing for a second straight month. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index was up 1.6% (1.0 point) to 61.7 this month. This is the index's highest level since February but still reflects historically low levels of optimism. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 7.1% (4.7 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month - a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment improved for the second straight month, inching up a scant single index point from June. Current conditions rose about 5% to its highest reading since February 2025, while the expectations index fell slightly. A rise in sentiment among stock holders was partially offset by a decline among consumers who do not own stocks. Perceptions of this month’s economic developments were similar across the political spectrum; Republicans, Independents, and Democrats all saw some minor increases in sentiment this month. Although recent trends show sentiment moving in a favorable direction, sentiment remains broadly negative. Consumers are hardly optimistic about the trajectory of the economy, even as their worries have softened since April 2025.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 61.7, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 26.9% below its average reading of 84.4 (arithmetic mean) and 25.9% below its geometric mean of 83.3, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 6th percentile of the 571 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 1.0-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In July, the CECI rose to 68.0, its second consecutive monthly increase and the highest level since January. This represents a 4.9% increase from the previous month and an 8.5% rise from a year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 66.8.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell to 57.7, its first monthly decline since April. This represents a 0.7% decrease from the prior month and a 16.1% decline from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 58.6.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations fell for a second straight month, plunging from 5.0% last month to 4.5% this month. This is the lowest reading since February 2025 but above December 2024 just after the election. Long-run inflation expectations receded for the third consecutive month, falling back from 4.0% in June to 3.4% in July. This is the lowest reading since January 2025 but, again, still considerably higher than the December 2024 reading.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Key ETF Asset Class Performance
data.description:
The market ended the month of July on a down note, but the S&P 500 (SPY) still ended the month with a gain of 2.3%. The Dow (DIA) only gained 0.2%, however, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) gained 2.4%. While the cap-weighted S&P gained more than 2%, the equal-weight version of the index (RSP) gained just 1%.

Above is a look at recent performance across asset classes using our key ETF matrix. For each ETF, we show its year-to-date change through July, its performance in July, and its performance on the first trading day of August.

While domestic equities posted small gains in July, there was quite a bit of disparity among international markets. The all-world ex-US ETF (CWI) actually fell 1.1% in July, while countries like Brazil (EWZ), France (EWQ), Germany (EWG), India (INDA), Japan (EWJ), and Israel (EIS) fell even more than that.

China (MCHI) and Hong Kong (EWH) went the other way with gains of more than 4% during the month. On the year, China (MCHI) finished July up 23.75% year-to-date, which is much better than SPY's 8.5% gain. Notably, India (INDA) ended July exactly flat on the year, which trails basically every other country around the world in terms of 2025 equity market returns.

Getting back to the US, sector returns have been scattered recently, with areas like Consumer Staples (XLP) and Health Care (XLV) selling off in July, while Industrials (XLI), Tech (XLK), and Utilities (XLU) - all areas benefitting from the AI Boom and its build-out - rallied.

To start August, however, Consumer Staples (XLP) and Health Care (XLV) are holding up well while the rest of the market plunges on August 1.

In terms of large caps versus small caps, yet again, the small-cap Russell 2000 (IWM) is lagging. IWM is down 2.1% today after finishing July in the red on a year-to-date basis. The small-cap value ETF (IJS) ended July down 6.4%. That's nearly 15 percentage points weaker than the 8.5% gain seen for the large-cap SPY. Will small caps ever outperform again?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TUSI: ABS Focused Cash Parking Vehicle
data.description:
Thesis

Touchstone Ultra Short Income ETF (BATS:TUSI) is a fixed income exchange traded fund. The name comes from Touchstone Investments and has the following objective:

While the fund may invest in securities with any maturity or duration, interest rate risk is managed by maintaining an effective duration of one year or less under normal market conditions. Duration is a reasonably accurate measure of a debt security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates and a common measure of interest rate risk. A lower duration indicates a lower sensitivity to interest rates. The fund invests primarily in investment grade debt securities.

We have not covered this name before, and in today's article we are going to look at the fund, its composition, its risk analytics and performance, and outline our view on the name in today's macro environment.

Fund composition

As per its stated objective, the fund is composed of investment grade securities:

AAA assets make up most of the fund at 47% of the holdings, followed by A and AA securities with a 13.5% and 12.5% weighting respectively. The fund has a very small 1.4% bucket for below investment grade securities, mostly fallen angels.

The asset parsing is overweight asset backed securities:

ABS make up 37.9% of the fund, followed by non-agency CMBS and IG corporates. This ETF is not your usual corporate fund, with a large allocation to securitizations.

Let us look now at some fund analytics:

  • AUM: $0.2 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.25%
  • Nr. of holdings: 180
  • Duration: 0.5 years
  • Turnover rate: 85%

The name is true to its short duration goal, with a very short 0.5-year duration, which has kept its risk profile very low. The fund risk analytics can be found on the 'Risk' page on the Seeking Alpha platform. The name has an annualized volatility of 1.2% and a standard deviation of 0.5%. These figures are computed on a 1-yer time-frame.

Performance

Now that we have a better understanding of how the fund is constructed and some of its risk analytics, let us see how the name has done historically. Please note the name is fairly recent, having IPO-ed in 2022. Its performance is as follows:

We are comparing TUSI to a cohort of peers, including T Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX), the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 month Tbill ETF (BIL) and the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT). TBUX and TUSI have the best total returns here, followed by MINT and BIL. We did expect BIL to be dead last since the name runs no credit risk via its T-Bills composition.

TUSI is a robust performer, and we are using a total return graph here in order to compare apples to apples. Let us now have a look at the fund's drawdown in 2025, especially in light of the risk-off market in April 2025:

The figures here are very, very compelling, with a mere -0.4% drawdown during April 2025. Kindly keep in mind that certain other parking vehicles had -1% to -2% drawdowns during that period. TUSI did very well, thus explaining its very low volatility figures.

It is very important for an investor to look at drawdowns. If an instrument, say, offers a 0.5% incremental total return over T-bills but has a -2% drawdown during market risk-off conditions, then it is not really a robust risk/reward vehicle. Excess yields need to be considered in tandem with drawdowns to get a better sense of the incremental risks taken.

Yield considerations

TUSI falls in the cash parking vehicles category, thus we are going to have a look at what the fund yields:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 4.45%

Although the fund has a SEC yield only marginally over T-bills, we have seen from its historic performance and its high turnover that the name is able to clip small capital gains, even under tough market conditions in order to generate a high total return.

What we like about this name is the fact that it is ABS weighted rather than corporate focused. Very many names tend to increase their yields via short dated corporate spreads. Not here. We find TUSI to be a portfolio diversificator from that perspective.

In times of turmoil, cash is king

Investors need to remember that cash is king, and more importantly it represents a hedge. While many retail investors think of options or inverse funds as hedges, simply sitting in cash also qualifies. Under a theoretical example where an investor is 100% in cash (or cash equivalents like TUSI) they are bound to have a flat performance even when the market is down -20%. While not a 'high-reward' holding, TUSI does do the job of providing an incremental yield over T-bills while holding its value with a low volatility profile.

Conclusion

TUSI is an interesting fixed income ETF. The name constitutes a cash parking vehicle and is overweight ABS bonds and non-Agency CMBS securities. The fund has done very well in 2025, with a very low -0.4% drawdown during the April 2025 market melt-down. We like the fund's high total returns and low volatility, as well as its composition as a diversification away from corporate bond focused names. We consider TUSI a robust cash parking vehicle and an underappreciated hedge in an ever volatile environment with stretched valuations.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii: Repricing Dynamics Continue To Drive Growth
data.description:
These past few months have been pretty soft for Bank of Hawaii (NYSE:BOH), with shares of this leading Hawaiian lender underperforming the wider regional bank space by around fifteen points since my last update in March. Things are at least going better at the business level for BOH, as its earnings continue to benefit from the ongoing repricing of its relatively high stock of fixed-rate assets.

That was one element of my bullish case for the bank. More generally, BOH's lower-risk lending also makes it a suitable candidate for the more risk-averse investor, which may be something to chew on given the ongoing uncertainty stemming from U.S. tariffs.

After recently releasing H1 2025 results, now seems like a good time to revisit the investment case here. I remain bullish, with BOH still trading at a discount based on its past-average P/E and P/TBV multiples.

Repricing Dynamics Driving Growth

For the first half of 2025, BOH earned $81.084 million in net income available to common shareholders ($2.03 per share), up sharply from the $66.536 million ($1.68 per share) it booked in H1 2024.

Quarterly profits are also improving, with net income available to common shareholders clocking in at $42.368 million ($1.06 per share) for Q2, up from $32.114 million ($0.81 per share) in the year-ago quarter and $38.716 million ($0.97 per share) in Q1. Q2 also marked BOH's fourth straight quarter of EPS growth.

This growth is largely being fueled by an improving net interest margin [NIM]. As I laid out last time, BOH was hit pretty hard during the latter stages of the hiking cycle. This was largely a result of its highly rigid balance sheet structure, with long-term fixed-rate mortgages and investment securities accounting for a relatively high share of its assets.

On the other side of the ledger, deposit mix-shift and the pass-through of the Fed's interest rate hikes also ate into BOH's deposit margin, the net result being a pronounced squeeze in its overall NIM.

Back to the present, and repricing dynamics continue to be much more favorable for BOH. On the liability side, its quarterly average cost of deposits has fallen around 27 bps from its 2024 peak. While that does imply a relatively weak cycle-to-date pass-through rate (the Fed has cut by 100 bps in total), this is really a function of BOH's already low-cost funding base. Put simply, BOH's cost of deposits increases less than at other banks when interest rates rise, which also means it can't cut as aggressively when rates fall.

Absent more cuts from the Fed, there isn't much left to squeeze out of this. Deposit costs were roughly flat quarter-on-quarter in Q2, and while BOH does have around $1.5 billion in time deposits falling off the books in Q3 at a blended yield above its prevailing offering, it is also still seeing a small headwind from mix-shift.

The other side of the balance sheet is a different story, as BOH's blended interest-earning asset yield was still only 4.01% in Q2. Of the roughly $22 billion BOH holds in interest-earning assets, investment securities account for a chunky 34%, and these were still only yielding 2.5% last quarter. Per management, the bank is currently putting maturities to work at a rate of ~5.4%.

Similarly, fixed-rate loans are falling off the book at a sub-5% blended yield, while yields on new loans are yielding closer to 7%. Weighing all of that up, fixed cashflows are maturing at a circa 4% average rate, with the current reinvestment rate around 230 bps above that.

It is true that volume growth remains more elusive. Deposits and earning-assets were only up by around 2% year-on-year last quarter, and loan growth was even weaker (~1%). That isn't a major surprise, as BOH's loan book does skew around one-third to residential mortgages, demand for which is suffering in the current interest rate environment. Looking at some Hawaiian housing stats, closed sales fell again year-on-year in H1, and they remain down sharply on pre-2023 levels.

Still, NIM expansion can do a fair bit of lifting on its own - and BOH's NIM expanded 7 bps sequentially last quarter. That was good for around 3% sequential growth in net interest income, and with operating costs flat quarter-on-quarter, the resulting operating leverage drove around 7% sequential growth in pre-provision profit.

Per the image from earlier, cashflow repricing is driving around $3.2 million in quarterly NII growth for BOH. While deposit mix-shift was claiming around 15% of that last quarter, the net impact is still positive to the tune of around 5 bps in terms of its NIM. Accordingly, management mentioned a 2025 exit NIM of around 2.50% on the earnings call, roughly 10 bps above its Q2 level.

It depends on how far back you go, but BOH's NIM has historically averaged around 2.8%. While repricing is going to be a long-term process, it should nevertheless be an ongoing tailwind for the bank's earnings.

Still Well-Placed For Uncertain Times

While tariff-related worries do seem to have calmed since earlier in the year, there is still a great deal of uncertainty out there. When I last covered BOH, I said that it was arguably as well-placed as any bank for a spell of economic turbulence. This is largely a function of its loan book, which is dominated by secured real estate. Per the chart below, this has historically resulted in a lower net loss rate for BOH compared to the average bank.

Like other banks, BOH isn't really seeing any concerning trends in its asset quality right now. Net charge-offs were only 7bps of total loans last quarter, which is right in line with its 2019-2024 average. Early-stage delinquencies also remain modest, with loans 30-59 days past due accounting for 0.18% of total loans at the end of Q2. This is down from around 22bps at the start of the year.

The Valuation

BOH trades for $61.35 as I type, which is equal to around 13.8x consensus 2025 EPS and 1.78x the bank's tangible book value at the end of Q2 ($34.37 per share).

These are rich-looking multiples for a sleepy regional bank, albeit there are some caveats. In terms of its earnings multiple, we have seen that balance sheet repricing is driving solid growth right now. As a result, BOH's P/E ratio falls to a more modest 12x based on 2026 consensus EPS.

In terms of its balance sheet multiple, BOH's tangible book value is somewhat depressed by the sizable unrealized losses on its investment securities. Excluding these losses, the stock's P/TBV multiple would be around 1.5x.

As you can see from the chart above, BOH's current multiples actually represent discounts to their historical averages. The dividend yield, currently over 4.5%, is also cheap relative to its past-average mark.

Conclusion

Bank of Hawaii has clocked in a disappointing return these past few months, even as trends at the business level continue to head in the right direction. Asset repricing should continue to be a tailwind for some time to come, while more generally, the bank's favorable credit loss record should appeal to the more conservative-minded investor. With the stock trading at a discount to its past-average multiples, I remain bullish.

data.title: C3.ai: Take Advantage Of CEO Volatility To Buy
data.description:
As we move deeper through the Q2 earnings season, the S&P 500 appears to be struggling to hold on to recent market highs, despite strong earnings from the tech sector that seems to defy a shaky macroeconomy. In this environment, I’m continuing to focus on rotating out of this year’s momentum winners and more into value-oriented, distressed corners of the market.

It’s tough to imagine that any AI software stock could be in distress, but that’s the case for C3.ai (NYSE:AI), the enterprise applications platform that began in 2009 as a simpler application for energy companies to track carbon emissions. Founder and CEO Tom Siebel, who is highly regarded in the industry, announced he was stepping down due to health reasons - and since the announcement was made, C3.ai has dropped ~10%, bringing year to date losses to more than 30%.

It’s an unprecedented reversal for a company that has exhibited strong revenue growth and verifiable AI investment tailwinds. I last wrote a buy opinion on C3.ai in May, when the stock was trading at $27 per share. Now at fresh new lows amid strong fundamental progress, I’m upgrading the stock to a strong buy.

Overblown concerns over loss of CEO

Let’s begin with a discussion of Tom Siebel’s exit. Siebel announced earlier this year that he was diagnosed with an autoimmune disease, causing visual impairment and restricting his ability to travel.

Needless to say, the market had a lot of respect for Siebel, especially given his track record (the sale of his first company, Siebel Systems), to Oracle in 2006 for $5.8 billion minted Siebel a billionaire). Clearly, some portion of C3.ai’s trading value was predicated on the hopes that Siebel would lead C3.ai to a similar exit (though some analysts also think a CEO switch increases, not decreases C3.ai’s chances of a buyout. I’m neutral here: I don’t necessarily think C3.ai is a near-term acquisition target, even though it’s cheap - and I don’t base my buy decision on this stock at all due to the possibility of a buyout).

But here’s what makes C3.ai’s leadership transition slightly different from other software leadership changes. Software investors have always been very nervous about when a CEO or particularly a sales head (often with the title of COO, CRO, or Sales EVP) leaves because it causes friction in the sales ranks and may disturb deals in progress.

While we won’t say that leadership turnover won’t produce similar effects for C3.ai, we will note that C3.ai relies much less on its internal sales network than other software peers. As shown in the chart below, in FY25, partner-led deals were responsible for closing 73% of C3.ai’s deals in the year.

Here’s the more literal translation: business will go on, as insensitive as that may sound in the wake of the founder’s health-related issues.

We think C3.ai’s competitive advantages lie in the breadth and access that its partner network provides. As a reminder, last quarter C3.ai announced a renewal of the reseller agreement with its oldest partner, Baker Hughes - fears of the cancellation of this contract were originally what drove downside in C3.ai’s stock earlier this year.

But C3.ai is going beyond just renewing these old agreements - its new partnership with Microsoft Azure also gives the company access to Microsoft’s tens of thousands of sales reps. To me, as shown in the chart above, the company’s 419% y/y growth in partner-led bookings is concrete evidence of the value that the Microsoft agreement, which only kicked off this year, is bringing to C3.ai’s sales momentum.

Separately - we think Microsoft's very strong recent earnings print, driven by its powerhouse Azure segment, is a very positive read-through for C3.ai that is not fully appreciated in C3.ai's recent share price declines.

Another advantage of the partner model is the fact that it’s enabling C3.ai to leverage its R&D investments and monetize its product without significant sales outlays - which is often what drives so much red ink for earlier stage software companies. In FY25 (the fiscal year for C3.ai ending in April), the company hit $22.2 million in free cash flow (a 6% margin), doubling y/y.

To me, this surge in cash flow demonstrates the operating leverage that C3.ai’s partner-led sales enables - and also helps to quell fears on the bear camp that C3.ai is a loss leader that is doomed to stay unprofitable.

Very appealing valuation

The core reason to buy C3.ai can be summed up in a simple statement: it’s way too cheap for a high-growth software company, let alone one that is a true AI play.

At current share prices near $23, C3.ai trades at a market cap of $3.17 billion. After we net off the $742.7 million of cash on the company's latest Q4 balance sheet, the stock’s resulting enterprise value is $2.43 billion.

For FY26 (the year ending next April 2026), C3.ai has guided to a rather wide range of 15-25% y/y revenue growth to $447.5-$484.5 million in revenue.

Against the midpoint of that outlook, C3.ai trades at just 5.2x EV/FY26 revenue. Meanwhile, when we stack up C3.ai against other software stocks that are expected to grow at a ~20% pace, we find that none of them trade at C3.ai's deeply discounted multiples: most are hovering just under double-digit multiple, while outliers like ServiceNow (NOW) are trading in the teens.

To me, this is an opportunity to scoop up C3.ai at a broad discount, while any firmer news on CEO succession should clear up the pessimism in the stock (just as resolution of the Baker Hughes renewal brought an immediate rebound to C3.ai).

Risks and key takeaways

To me, the core risks that C3.ai faces have little to do with Tom Siebel's exit, and more to do with execution. It probably goes without saying that the AI landscape is shifting rapidly at the moment, and based on market activity alone, it seems that Wall Street has decreed that Palantir (PLTR) is a winner and C3.ai is a loser. Both companies, meanwhile, provide AI-driven, turnkey software applications to address a wide range of industries and functional needs. That being said, C3.ai will face sharp competition for years to come, and we'll need to monitor its ability to maintain market share as the AI industry advances rapidly.

C3.ai's reliance on partners also brings risk, alongside the benefits previously mentioned. The company faces the risk of becoming overly reliant on Microsoft Azure's sales team, and recognizing this leverage, Microsoft could negotiate more favorable terms from C3.ai (knowing full well that a potential "breakup" could be jarring for the company, as history tells us from Baker Hughes).

That being said, I think these risks are already more than well reflected into C3.ai's bargain stock at ~5x revenue, which is about half the revenue multiple of most similarly-growing peers. In my view, it's best to take advantage of recent pessimism as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. Services PMI, Bank Of England Rate Decision And Canadian/NZ Employment
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Volatile week between trade deals, the FOMC meeting, and the non farm payrolls report

The week kicked off with a risk-positive tone as headlines around a Euro-US trade breakthrough triggered a sharp gap higher in global markets. As a result, the US dollar caught a strong bid, with the euro and yen notably losing ground amid improving US trade positioning and capital rotation into USD assets.

Midweek, the FOMC held rates steady as expected, but the tone was less dovish than markets hoped. With no signal for a September cut, rate futures quickly repriced, sending the dollar even higher and pressuring risk assets globally. The move extended a USD run that was already in motion.

The dollar index, which touched 97.20 last Wednesday, ripped through the 100 handle, peaking near 100.20 by Friday morning. That strength started to shake broader markets, especially in FX, commodities, and rate-sensitive sectors.

Then came the NFP report. A messy release with mixed revisions sent shockwaves across asset classes. Bonds and gold rallied hard on safe-haven flows, while equities took a hit as traders digested the combo of slowing growth and hawkish policy repricing. It seems that the Goldilocks conditions for stocks have found some resistance.

Earnings Season

The strong earnings season kept on providing good results, with notable releases from 4 of the Magnificent 7, including Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT). This has kept boosting the Nasdaq and S&P 500 towards their most recent all-time highs before the mood got dampened by this morning's data.

The Week Ahead: Less market-moving than last week, but emphasis on US Services PMIs and the Bank of England rate decision

Last week was a composed salad of key data, particularly for the US - Between ADP employment, the July FOMC, GDP, Core PCE, and finally this morning's NFP.

Now, markets are on edge going into the weekend after a reality check that things are not all so easy in the end. Firms face the consequences of the Trump administration's policies, which will keep influencing the data as the August 1 deadline bell rings.

Asia Pacific Markets - US/China Trade Talks pursue, NZ employment data

This week saw the further pushback of US-China trade talks for 90 more days, leaving markets awaiting again for more concrete developments between the strongest nation and the world's largest manufacturer.

In terms of key data for APAC nations, we will be expecting the Caixin Services PMIs (Monday evening) and the Inflation Data (Friday evening) from the Middle Kingdom.

NZD traders will await their employment numbers on Tuesday end-afternoon and some more key releases with Wednesday's RBNZ Inflation Expectations survey for Q3. AUD traders will also have to be attentive to the Australian trade balance numbers, also releasing on Wednesday evening.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and the US

The EU will not provide much high-tier data for markets, so traders will have to look elsewhere for Euro movement. Focus on US dollar demand (or lack thereof) if you want to get a clear picture of FX flows.

For the US, the most important data of the week is expected on Tuesday at 10:00 A.M. with the ISM Services PMI release (consensus 51.5, previous 50.8).

The Bank of England notably releases their rate decision on Thursday, August 7th morning, with a cut from 4.25% to 4% largely expected.

CAD traders will have to take a look for three important data points next week: the Canadian Trade Balance (releasing on Tuesday), both Services and Manufacturing PMIs releasing on Wednesday, and finally, the Canadian employment data on Friday.

Don't forget the key earnings that equity markets are awaiting with names like McDonald's (MCD), Palantir (PLTR), Disney (DIS), and Pfizer (PFE).

Safe trades and enjoy your weekend, as next week should be another rollercoaster!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
